annotations.label: Number theory

anchor.snippet: Number theory is the branch of mathematics that studies integers

annotations.note: A branch of mathematics, not a formalizable mathematical statement.

annotations.label: Integers

anchor.snippet: The integers comprise a set that extends the set of natural numbers

annotations.note: The integer type `Int` (`ℤ`) is a Lean core definition used throughout Mathlib.

annotations.label: Elementary number theory

anchor.snippet: Elementary number theory studies aspects of integers

annotations.note: A subfield of mathematics, not a formalizable statement.

annotations.label: Geometric number theory

anchor.snippet: Geometric number theory uses concepts from geometry to study numbers

annotations.note: A subfield of mathematics, not a formalizable statement.

annotations.label: Pythagorean triples (Plimpton 322)

anchor.snippet: a list of Pythagorean triples , that is, integers

annotations.note: `PythagoreanTriple x y z` is defined as `x^2 + y^2 = z^2` over the integers.

anchors.snippet: a list of Pythagorean triples , that is, integers

annotations.label: Diophantine equations

anchor.snippet: Diophantine equations are polynomial equations to which rational or integer solutions are sought

annotations.note: Mathlib treats specific Diophantine equations (Pell, FLT) but has no general 'Diophantine equation' object.

annotations.label: Arithmetic

anchor.snippet: Arithmetic is the study of numerical operations

annotations.note: A descriptive notion of a field of study, not a formalizable statement.

annotations.label: Multiplication

anchor.snippet: Multiplication, for instance, is an operation that combines two numbers

annotations.note: Multiplication is given by the core `Mul`/`HMul` typeclass, instantiated for `ℤ` and `ℕ`.

annotations.label: Divisibility

anchor.snippet: Divisibility is a property between two nonzero integers related to division

annotations.note: The divisibility relation `∣` is the core `Dvd` typeclass, available on `ℤ`.

annotations.label: Euclid's division lemma

anchor.snippet: Euclid's division lemma asserts that

annotations.note: `Nat.div_add_mod` (with `Nat.mod_lt`) gives the division-with-remainder decomposition; `Int.emod_add_ediv` is the integer analogue.

annotations.label: Divisibility rule for 3

anchor.snippet: any integer is divisible by 3 if its decimal digit sum is divisible by 3

annotations.note: `Nat.three_dvd_iff` states `3 ∣ n ↔ 3 ∣ (digits 10 n).sum`.

annotations.label: Common divisor

anchor.snippet: A common divisor of several nonzero integers is an integer that divides all of them

annotations.note: There is no standalone 'common divisor' predicate; only `Dvd` and `gcd` exist.

annotations.label: Greatest common divisor

anchor.snippet: The greatest common divisor (gcd) is the largest of such divisors

annotations.note: `Nat.gcd` (and `GCDMonoid.gcd`) give the greatest common divisor, characterized by `Nat.gcd_dvd`/`Nat.dvd_gcd`.

annotations.label: Coprime integers

anchor.snippet: Two integers are said to be coprime or relatively prime to one another

annotations.note: `Nat.Coprime m n` is defined as `gcd m n = 1`; `IsCoprime` is the general ring version.

annotations.label: Euclidean algorithm

anchor.snippet: The Euclidean algorithm computes the greatest common divisor of two integers

annotations.note: `Nat.gcd` and `EuclideanDomain.gcd` are defined by the Euclidean recursion, with Bézout as `Nat.gcd_eq_gcd_ab`.

annotations.label: Diophantine equation

anchor.snippet: A Diophantine equation has several unknowns and integer coefficients

annotations.note: No general 'Diophantine equation' definition exists in Mathlib, only specific instances.

annotations.label: Pythagorean triples

anchor.snippet: whose solutions are called Pythagorean triples if they are all integers

annotations.note: `PythagoreanTriple x y z := x^2 + y^2 = z^2` over `ℤ`, with full parametrization proved.

annotations.label: Continued fraction

anchor.snippet: Another kind of expression is the continued fraction

annotations.note: `GenContFract` formalizes (generalized) continued fractions, with convergence results.

annotations.label: Prime number

anchor.snippet: A prime number is an integer greater than

annotations.note: `Nat.Prime` defines primality on `ℕ`; `Prime` is the general commutative-monoid version.

annotations.label: Composite number

anchor.snippet: that is not prime is called a composite number

annotations.note: Mathlib has no named 'composite' predicate; it is expressed via `¬ Nat.Prime` together with `1 < n`.

annotations.label: Euclid's theorem (infinitude of primes)

anchor.snippet: Euclid's theorem demonstrates that there are infinitely many prime numbers

annotations.note: `Nat.exists_infinite_primes` shows for every `n` there is a prime `p ≥ n`.

annotations.label: Sieve of Eratosthenes

anchor.snippet: The sieve of Eratosthenes was devised as an efficient algorithm for identifying all primes

annotations.note: The Eratosthenes sieving algorithm is not formalized (Mathlib's SelbergSieve is a different analytic tool).

annotations.label: Factorization

anchor.snippet: Factorization is a method of expressing a number as a product

annotations.note: `Nat.factorization` records a number's prime-power exponents as a finitely supported function.

annotations.label: Integer factorization

anchor.snippet: integer factorization is the decomposition of an integer into a product of integers

annotations.note: Decomposition into a product is captured by `Nat.factorization` / `UniqueFactorizationMonoid`.

annotations.label: Prime factorization

anchor.snippet: is known as prime factorization

annotations.note: `Nat.primeFactorsList` is the list of prime factors with `prod_primeFactorsList` recovering `n`.

annotations.label: Euclid's lemma

anchor.snippet: if a prime divides a product of integers, then that prime divides at least one of the factors

annotations.note: `Nat.Prime.dvd_mul`/`Prime.dvd_or_dvd` state `p ∣ m*n ↔ p ∣ m ∨ p ∣ n`.

annotations.label: Fundamental theorem of arithmetic

anchor.snippet: The theorem states that every integer greater than

annotations.note: `Nat.primeFactorsList_unique` (with `UniqueFactorizationMonoid.factors_unique`) gives existence and uniqueness of prime factorization.

annotations.label: Unique prime factorization example

anchor.snippet: is expressed uniquely as

annotations.note: A specific numeric factorization example; no named decl, though provable by `decide`/`norm_num`.

annotations.label: Modular arithmetic

anchor.snippet: Modular arithmetic works with finite sets of integers

annotations.note: `ZMod n` is the ring of integers modulo `n`.

anchors.snippet: Modular arithmetic works with finite sets of integers

annotations.label: Congruence

anchor.snippet: A congruence of two integers

annotations.note: `Int.ModEq n a b` (`a ≡ b [ZMOD n]`) defines congruence; `Nat.ModEq` is the natural-number version.

annotations.label: Residue class

anchor.snippet: is a set that contains all integers congruent to a specified

annotations.note: Residue classes mod `n` are exactly the elements of `ZMod n`.

anchors.snippet: is a set that contains all integers congruent to a specified

annotations.label: Fermat's little theorem

anchor.snippet: An influential theorem is Fermat's little theorem

annotations.note: `ZMod.pow_card` gives `a^p = a`, with `ZMod.pow_card_sub_one_eq_one` the `a^(p-1)=1` form.

anchors.snippet: An influential theorem is Fermat's little theorem

annotations.label: Euler's theorem

anchor.snippet: Euler's theorem extends this to assert that every integer

annotations.note: `Nat.ModEq.pow_totient` states `x^φ(n) ≡ 1 [MOD n]` for `x` coprime to `n`.

anchors.snippet: Euler's theorem extends this to assert that every integer

annotations.label: Analytic number theory

anchor.snippet: Analytic number theory, in contrast to elementary number theory

annotations.note: A subfield of mathematics, not a formalizable statement.

annotations.label: Analysis (limit)

anchor.snippet: Analysis is the branch of mathematics that studies the limit

annotations.note: The limit notion is formalized via `Filter.Tendsto`, but 'analysis' as a field is not a statement.

annotations.label: Limit of a sequence

anchor.snippet: For example, the limit of the sequence

annotations.note: Limits of sequences are expressed as `Filter.Tendsto u atTop (nhds L)`.

annotations.label: Complex numbers and imaginary unit

anchor.snippet: The complex numbers extend the real numbers with the imaginary unit

annotations.note: `Complex` (`ℂ`) and the imaginary unit `Complex.I` (with `I^2 = -1`) are defined.

annotations.label: Real and imaginary parts

anchor.snippet: Every complex number can be expressed as

annotations.note: `Complex.re` and `Complex.im` give the real and imaginary parts of a complex number.

annotations.label: Prime-counting function

anchor.snippet: The distribution of primes , described by the function

annotations.note: `Nat.primeCounting` (notation `π`) counts primes ≤ n.

annotations.label: Prime number theorem

anchor.snippet: The prime number theorem in analytic number theory provides a formalisation

annotations.note: The prime number theorem is proved in the external PrimeNumberTheoremAnd project, not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Riemann zeta function

anchor.snippet: It is defined as the series

annotations.note: `riemannZeta` is the analytically continued zeta function, equal to `∑ 1/n^s` for `re s > 1`.

anchors.snippet: It is defined as the series

annotations.label: Euler product formula

anchor.snippet: Euler demonstrated a link involving the infinite product over all prime numbers

annotations.note: `riemannZeta_eulerProduct` proves the product over primes equals `ζ(s)` for `re s > 1`.

anchors.snippet: Euler demonstrated a link involving the infinite product over all prime numbers

annotations.label: Riemann hypothesis

anchor.snippet: Riemann extended the definition to a complex variable and conjectured

annotations.note: The statement `RiemannHypothesis` is formalized as a `Prop` (unproven conjecture).

anchors.snippet: Riemann extended the definition to a complex variable and conjectured

annotations.label: Prime ideals

anchor.snippet: one may define prime ideals (generalizations of prime numbers in the field of algebraic numbers)

annotations.note: `Ideal.IsPrime` is the standard prime-ideal class.

annotations.label: Dedekind zeta functions

anchor.snippet: Dedekind zeta functions , which are generalizations of the Riemann zeta function

annotations.note: `dedekindZeta` defines the Dedekind zeta function of a number field.

annotations.label: Elementary proof

anchor.snippet: Elementary number theory works with elementary proofs

annotations.note: A meta-mathematical notion about proof methods, not a formalizable statement.

annotations.label: Algebraic number

anchor.snippet: An algebraic number is any complex number that is a solution to some polynomial equation

annotations.note: `IsAlgebraic R x` (e.g. over `ℚ`) means `x` is a root of a nonzero polynomial; `Transcendental` is its negation.

annotations.label: Algebraic number field

anchor.snippet: Fields of algebraic numbers are also called algebraic number fields

annotations.note: `NumberField K` is a finite-dimensional field extension of `ℚ`.

annotations.label: Algebraic number theory

anchor.snippet: Algebraic number theory studies algebraic number fields

annotations.note: A subfield of mathematics, not a formalizable statement.

annotations.label: Quadratic field

anchor.snippet: A quadratic field consists of all numbers of the form

annotations.note: `Zsqrtd d` formalizes `ℤ[√d]` with integer coefficients; the rational quadratic field `ℚ(√d)` is not a dedicated definition.

annotations.label: Field extension

anchor.snippet: a field L is said to be an extension of a field K if L contains K

annotations.note: Field extensions are modeled by `[Field K] [Field L] [Algebra K L]` and `IntermediateField`, with no single 'field extension' predicate.

annotations.label: Abelian extension

anchor.snippet: Abelian extensions—that is, extensions L of K such that the Galois group

annotations.note: `IsAbelianGalois K L` is a Galois extension with commutative Galois group.

annotations.label: Central problem of Diophantine geometry

anchor.snippet: The central problem of Diophantine geometry is to determine when a Diophantine equation has integer or rational solutions

annotations.note: A description of an open research problem, not a formalizable statement.

annotations.label: Rational and integral points

anchor.snippet: one asks whether there are any rational points (points all of whose coordinates are rationals)

annotations.note: No general notion of rational/integral points on a variety is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Rational points on the unit circle

anchor.snippet: Consider, for instance, the Pythagorean equation

annotations.note: The Pythagorean-triple classification parametrizes the rational points of the unit circle, but they are not stated as such.

annotations.label: Finiteness of points depends on genus

anchor.snippet: depends crucially on the genus of the curve

annotations.note: The Mordell conjecture (Faltings's theorem) is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Diophantine approximation

anchor.snippet: There is also the closely linked area of Diophantine approximations

annotations.note: Dirichlet's approximation theorem and related results are formalized, but 'Diophantine approximation' as an area is not a single statement.

annotations.label: Good rational approximation

anchor.snippet: a good approximation to

annotations.note: This theorem captures 'good approximations' `|ξ - p/q| < 1/q^2`, infinitely many for irrational `ξ`.

annotations.label: Transcendental number

anchor.snippet: if a number can be approximated better than any algebraic number, then it is a transcendental number

annotations.note: `Transcendental R x` means `x` is not algebraic; `Liouville.transcendental` realizes the approximation criterion.

annotations.label: π and e are transcendental

anchor.snippet: It is by this argument that π and e have been shown to be transcendental

annotations.note: Only the analytical part of the Lindemann–Weierstrass theorem is in Mathlib; transcendence of `π` and `e` is not yet a completed theorem.

annotations.label: Geometry of numbers

anchor.snippet: Diophantine geometry should not be confused with the geometry of numbers

annotations.note: Minkowski's fundamental theorem, the core of the geometry of numbers, is formalized, though the field itself is not a statement.

annotations.label: Arithmetic geometry

anchor.snippet: Arithmetic geometry is a contemporary term for the same domain covered by Diophantine geometry

annotations.note: A subfield of mathematics, not a formalizable statement.

annotations.label: Probabilistic number theory

anchor.snippet: Probabilistic number theory starts with questions such as the following

annotations.note: A subfield of mathematics, not a formalizable statement.

annotations.label: Combinatorial number theory

anchor.snippet: Combinatorics in number theory starts with questions like the following

annotations.note: A subfield of mathematics, not a formalizable statement.
